participants.reasoning: The extracted text adds carers, health professionals, and a pilot sample from Sydney, whereas the official record describes only eligible patients in a randomized outpatient consultation study across 9 services with different recruitment details.
participants.extracted: Three participant groups were involved. In the qualitative development phase, 19 palliative care patients and 24 carers were recruited from hospitals, palliative care units, and community settings across three palliative care services in Sydney, Australia; eligible participants were adults over 18 years old, English-speaking, well enough to participate, able to give informed consent, and referred to specialist palliative care for an incurable progressive illness (or carers of such patients). In addition, 22 health professionals working in palliative care were interviewed from 10 services in two Australian states, including senior doctors, registrars, nurses, and allied health staff. A further 21 health professionals reviewed the draft question prompt list. The pilot study then enrolled 23 palliative care patients seeing one of three palliative care doctors in outpatient clinics in Sydney.
participants.official:
Participants: patients with diagnosis of an advanced progressive life limiting illness, English speaking, older than 18 years of age, and able and well enough to read QPL and complete questionnaires
Setting and geographic location: 9 Australian palliative care (PC) services in 2 states. Setting unclear but QPL was administered at time of consultation with physician. Almost all patients were recruited from outpatient clinics
Methods of recruitment: recruitment at the clinic of 15 PC physicians fr

interventions.reasoning: Both descriptions identify a question prompt list booklet for advanced cancer/palliative care patients, given shortly before outpatient palliative care consultations to encourage discussion of end-of-life and related concerns.
interventions.extracted: A structured question prompt list (QPL) booklet designed to support advance care planning and end-of-life communication in palliative care. The intervention was developed from patient, carer, and clinician interviews and reviewed by health professionals; it was provided to patients before outpatient palliative care consultations, with about 20 minutes to review it, and clinicians were asked to endorse and refer to it during the visit. The booklet was intended to help patients ask questions about their illness, prognosis, symptoms, support needs, carers' concerns, and end-of-life issues, thereby facilitating discussion and shared understanding during palliative care consultations.
interventions.official:
Intervention: QPL for advanced cancer patients and their caregivers, referred for palliative care (PC)
Aim of intervention: to determine whether provision of a QPL influences advanced cancer
patients’/caregivers’ questions and discussion of topics relevant to EoL care during consultations with a PC physician
Comparison: usual care (routine consultation with PC physician)
Delivered by: physicians
Setting: at clinic where consultation occurred in 9 outpatient PC clinics

outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes mention several post-consultation questionnaire measures and a follow-up at 3 weeks, but they omit key official primary outcomes such as total questions in the consultation, information preferences/needs, satisfaction, and actual versus preferred involvement, and they also do not match the official STAI timing at 24 hours after consultation.
outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure patient anxiety using the Spielberger State Anxiety Inventory (20-item state anxiety scale; score range 20–80, higher scores indicating greater anxiety) at two time points: immediately before the consultation after reviewing the question prompt list, and immediately after the consultation; a further anxiety-related follow-up questionnaire was administered at 3 weeks. The study also planned to measure patient responses to the question prompt list using a post-consultation questionnaire with standard items assessing perceived helpfulness, ease of asking questions, usefulness of the booklet, whether it helped patients put concerns into words, whether it was overwhelming or anxiety-provoking, whether it was easy to understand, adequacy of time to read it, preferred timing of receipt, whether it prompted questions for the doctor or other palliative care team members, whether others read it, and whether it was useful for subsequent contacts with the palliative care team at 3 weeks. Clinician-rated outcomes were also planned using post-consultation and end-of-study questionnaires asking whether the question prompt list interfered with the flow of the consultation and whether it made it easier for the patient or clinician to raise certain issues, as well as clinicians’ attitudes toward the booklet and willingness to use it in future practice.
outcomes.official:
Primary outcomes
Total number of questions during consultation [Discussions about EoL/EoL care]
Method: coding of audiotaped consultations
Timing: after consultation with PC physician
Scale and scoring: the QPL is a 16‐page A5 booklet containing 112 questions grouped into 9 topics encompassing issues that may be discussed with a physician or another health professional.
